issue: The server message handler is `async` and `await`s `EncounterSession.create()` (and collision/mask loading). The design's whole determinism story rests on "the server is a single-threaded JS event loop (serializes messages)", but `await` YIELDS the loop. While `proposeMove`/vote-resolution awaits `EncounterSession.create({...})` to build the encounter (combined design §4 resolution effect / index.ts:393), other queued messages for the SAME room (a second human's `proposeMove`, a `castVote`, an `equip`, even a `reset`) run to completion on the awaited gap. Two near-simultaneous vote resolutions can both pass the `room.vote === null`/`phase==='map'` guard before either sets `phase='combat'`, building TWO EncounterSessions and assigning `room.session` twice (last writer wins; the first session and its broadcast `combatStart` are orphaned, clients desync).
rationale: This is the single most load-bearing false assumption in the design. Every subsystem repeats "single-threaded, serializes messages, so no locking needed" — but the existing code already proves the gap is real (index.ts message handler is `async` and awaits create()). The vote/move-resolution path is exactly where two humans race, and it does async work mid-transition.
suggestedResolution: Make phase transition atomic BEFORE any await: set `room.phase='combat'` and `room.vote=null` (and a `room.building=true` guard) synchronously at the top of resolution, THEN `await EncounterSession.create()`. Reject/queue any room-scoped message that arrives while `room.building` is true. Generally: never mutate room-routing-relevant state across an await without a re-validation guard after the await resumes.

issue: `reclaim`/`hello` flips `seat.controller` from ai→human and removes the Sovereign brain (`session.heroBrains.delete(heroId)`) at message time, but a `driveAiSteps`/`runPlayerBots` burst may be mid-flight referencing that brain. The combat-design §5.4 explicitly drives player bots in a synchronous burst guarded by `aiPlayerBusy`, but reclaim handling (reconnect §9, room.ts §6) is described as mutating `occupant`+`heroBrains` "at message boundaries" — there is no stated check of `aiPlayerBusy`/`pendingDefend` before the flip. If the burst is paused on a defend prompt (control returned to the event loop), a reclaim message is processed, deletes the brain, and clears `seat.ready`; when the burst resumes it has a half-applied queue, or the now-human seat is double-driven (bot already queued its actions in `currentActions`/`actionIndex`).
rationale: The AiTurnRunner keeps mutable `currentActions`/`actionIndex`/`entityQueue` (ai-turn-runner.ts:23-29). A defendPrompt returns control to the loop (index.ts:199), so reclaim genuinely interleaves. The design hand-waves this as 'mutate only between steps' but never specifies the guard.
suggestedResolution: Gate reclaim's control-flip behind the same `aiPlayerBusy`/`pendingDefend` checks: if a bot burst or defend round is active for that seat, defer the brain swap until the runner reports `done`/the round resolves (set a `pendingReclaim` flag the runner drains). Never delete a heroBrain entry that the current `entityQueue` still references mid-burst.

issue: Two designs disagree on where ready/seat flags travel, and BOTH put a copy somewhere that can drift from the Room's canonical store. The wire-protocol design embeds `coop: CoopMeta` (with per-seat `passed`/`exhausted`/`connected`) INSIDE the serialized `GameState` snapshot (§5.1), while the combat design insists ready flags live ONLY in `Room.seats[].ready` and travel via a SEPARATE `phaseStatus` message, NOT in GameState (§2.1, §7). The client design assumes a third thing: `RoomStateDTO.seats[].ready` from `roomState`. These are three mutation paths for the same logical 'who has passed' state, broadcast on different triggers (every `state` vs every `phaseStatus` vs every `roomState`). A client can receive a `state` whose `coop.seats` says seat2 passed and a later/earlier `phaseStatus` that says it hasn't, with no ordering guarantee between the two message types.
rationale: This is a direct contradiction between the wire-protocol subsystem and the combat subsystem, not a subtle race. If implemented as written, two server code paths populate overlapping seat status into two message types with independent send triggers, guaranteeing transient inconsistency and 'waiting on X' UI flicker/deadlock-looking states.
suggestedResolution: Pick ONE carrier. Recommend the combat design's choice: ready/pass/connected live only in Room and travel only in `roomState`/`phaseStatus`; do NOT put `coop.seats[].passed/exhausted` in the serialized GameState. If a single snapshot is preferred, drop the separate `phaseStatus` message and make `coop` authoritative — but not both. Document a single send-trigger so ordering is well-defined.

issue: Phase-end (`playerPhaseDone()`) is recomputed 'after every applied player action and every setReady' (combat §3.3) and fires `endTurn` 'exactly once'. But the trigger points are reached from multiple message handlers (`action`, `pass`, `unpass`, the bot-burst `done` callback, and disconnect-flips-seat-to-bot-auto-ready). With async gaps (a human `action` whose path-resolve is sync but a disconnect `close` handler interleaving), two separate handlers can each observe `playerPhaseDone()===true` and each call `session.applyAction({type:'endTurn'})`. The resolver's `resolveEndTurn` (turn-resolver.ts:240) unconditionally flips activeTeam, so a double endTurn flips red→blue→red, skipping the enemy phase entirely (or running it twice).
rationale: `resolveEndTurn` has no idempotency/guard — it flips whatever `activeTeam` currently is. The design relies on 'exactly once' but the predicate is evaluated at many call sites including the disconnect path (which auto-readies a seat and could be the last ready). Nothing latches 'I already ended this phase'.
suggestedResolution: Latch the transition: gate the endTurn call on `room.phase`/a `phaseEnding` flag, or check `session.state.activeTeam === 'red'` immediately before calling endTurn and set a `playerPhaseEnded` boolean cleared only when the enemy phase flips back. Make disconnect's auto-ready route through the same single phase-end evaluator rather than calling endTurn directly.

issue: Defend-round aggregation (combat §6.3 `DefendRound`) builds `expected` (human seats) at the moment the prompt fans out and resolves when `expected` empties. But seat controller status can change DURING the round: a human in `expected` disconnects (→bot) — does the server auto-fill its result and remove it from `expected`? The disconnect handler (`close`) is a separate message path that flips the seat to bot but is not described as touching the live `DefendRound`. Conversely a bot-owned target was auto-resolved synchronously at round build, but if that seat is reclaimed by a human mid-round, the human's later `defendResult` for that promptId arrives for an already-resolved target. There is no stated promptId-vs-round reconciliation across a controller flip mid-round.
rationale: The combat design's own RISKS flag 'reclaim mid-defend' as med but the mitigation ('first answer wins, answered flag') only covers the wire-protocol design's per-prompt model — the combat design uses a Room-level `DefendRound` with an `expected` Set that the disconnect/reclaim handlers never update. A human in `expected` who disconnects deadlocks the round until the (unspecified) timeout.
suggestedResolution: On `close`, scan the active `DefendRound`: if the disconnecting seat is in `expected`, auto-fill its result (neutral multiplier) and remove it, then re-check completion. On reclaim, do NOT add a seat back into `expected` if its target already has a recorded result. Make the defend timeout server-authoritative and per-round (the wire design's open question #6) so a frozen/half-disconnected client can never stall the enemy sweep.

issue: `debugWin` and `reset` are host-gated and set `room.session.state.winner='red'` / tear down the session, but there is no stated guard against firing them while `driveAiSteps`/a bot burst is mid-flight or a `DefendRound` is open. In the current code `debugWin` directly mutates `session.state` (index.ts:460) and `reset` reassigns `session` (index.ts:501). Under the room model, host presses reset/debugWin while the enemy AI sweep is paused on a defend prompt: the session is replaced, but the AiTurnRunner closure still captures `getState/setState` over the OLD session, and any in-flight `defendResult` from a non-host seat resolves against a torn-down/replaced session.
rationale: AiTurnRunner is constructed per-session with closures over `this.state` (encounter-session.ts:42-47). Replacing `room.session` does not stop a runner that the event loop will re-enter when a queued `defendResult` arrives. Multi-human means a non-host can have a defend prompt outstanding exactly when the host resets.
suggestedResolution: Make reset/debugWin abort any open DefendRound and mark the old session/runner abandoned (a generation counter checked in driveAiSteps and resolveDefend). Reject `defendResult` whose promptId belongs to a superseded session generation.

issue: Per-seat inventory equip/unequip is allowed only while `phase !== 'combat'` per the overworld design (§3 'Equip is only allowed while room.phase !== combat'), but the combat-start transition (vote resolution → build encounter) reads each seat's loadout to bake into the hero. Because the vote-resolution `await EncounterSession.create()` yields the loop, an `equip` message that arrives in the gap — after `phase` was set to 'combat' but the guard is checked against the seat's own socket — is correctly rejected ONLY if `phase` was set BEFORE the await. The design sets phase inside `applyPartyMove` but the ordering of 'set phase=combat' vs 'read seat loadouts into SeatBuildSpec' vs 'await create' is unspecified, so a seat can equip a different weapon than the one baked into its hero, or two seats' loadouts are snapshotted at different times relative to the phase flip.
subsystem: per-seat loadout snapshot vs equip race
rationale: The combined design threads each seat's `getItemAbilities(seat.inventory.equipped)` into the builder per seat (overworld §3 last paragraph). If loadouts are read across the same await that builds the map, an interleaved equip mutates `seat.inventory` between the phase flip and the snapshot read.
suggestedResolution: Snapshot ALL seat loadouts into the immutable `SeatBuildSpec[]` array synchronously, and set `phase='combat'` synchronously, BEFORE the first await in encounter creation. Reject equip when `phase==='combat'` is then trivially correct because the flip happened pre-await.

issue: The vote electorate is defined inconsistently across the three overworld treatments, and the size H is computed at resolution time while membership can change mid-vote. Combined design §3.7 says 'all connected humans have voted'; overworld design §5 step 4 ties H to 'human-occupied seats (connected or disconnected-but-still-human? — see below)' and its open-question #5 leaves it unresolved; wire-protocol §4 says 'every connected human'. If a human disconnects (→bot) AFTER casting but BEFORE resolution, does their ballot still count, and does H shrink? A shrinking H can flip an already-cast tally (2 yes of 3 humans = majority; one no-voter disconnects → H=2, now 2 yes is unanimous and resolves; or the disconnecting voter was a 'no' and removing them flips to yes-majority). The tie-break ('proposer wins') interacts with this: a proposer who disconnects mid-vote both leaves the electorate AND vacates the tie-breaker.
rationale: Three sibling designs give three definitions and one explicitly flags it unresolved. Disconnect is a separate async message path that mutates seat controller; if `tryResolve` recomputes H from current human seats each call, a disconnect can silently change an in-progress tally's outcome.
suggestedResolution: Freeze the electorate at proposal time (snapshot the set of human seatIds into the MovementVote), or define resolution strictly over ballots-cast with a fixed denominator. Specify that a mid-vote disconnect drops that seat's ballot AND recomputes against the frozen electorate, and that a proposer disconnect cancels the vote (no orphaned tie-breaker). Pick ONE rule across all three subsystems.

issue: `aiPlayerBusy` rejects human actions during the player-bot burst (combat §5.4), but the burst is described as running 'in one server-driven burst at the start of the player phase'. A human action submitted just before the burst starts but processed just after `startPlayerPhase` sets `aiPlayerBusy=true` is rejected-with-ack — fine. But the burst calls `driveAiSteps` which is synchronous ONLY until a defend prompt; in the player-bots phase the design claims bots never pause for defend (red-bot attacking blue → no prompt). However if a red bot's AoE/attack hits ANOTHER red hero (friendly fire, or a zone effect), the `promptsDefense` predicate for a HUMAN-owned red target returns true, and the player-bot burst WOULD pause for a human defend prompt — re-entering the event loop with `aiPlayerBusy=true` held indefinitely while waiting on a human, during which all humans are locked out and the phase cannot end.
rationale: The design asserts player-bot turns are 'non-interactive' assuming bots only ever attack blue. Nothing in the resolver prevents a red attack from hitting red (resolveWeaponAttack filters by aim/geometry, not team — ai-turn-runner.ts:107 filters `teamId !== aiTeam`, and in playerBots mode aiTeam='red', so it would prompt for red targets... including OTHER red bots' and humans' heroes). A human-owned red hero caught in a bot's AoE triggers a real defend prompt mid-burst.
suggestedResolution: Either guarantee red bots never select friendly-fire-capable aims at allies (planner constraint), or explicitly handle a defend prompt arising during the bot burst: release `aiPlayerBusy` while awaiting the human's defendResult, then resume — and ensure phase-end logic tolerates the burst not yet being `done`. Add a test for a red AoE overlapping an ally hero.

issue: Reclaim-clears-ready policy is left as an explicit open question in two designs (combined design §4 'Optional refinement: clear ready on human reclaim...the turn engine owns that policy'; combat §5.4 'clears ready if the bot hadn't acted yet'). This is a phase-end race: if the player phase is open and all OTHER seats are ready, and a reclaiming human clears their (bot-set) ready, the phase that was about to end now re-opens. If the endTurn was already issued (the reclaim message and the last `pass` race), the reclaim arrives after activeTeam flipped to blue; clearing `ready` on a seat whose phase is already enemy-phase corrupts the next player phase's reset assumptions.
rationale: The 'who clears ready' policy is admittedly undecided and sits exactly on the phase boundary. With multiple humans, the last-pass and the reclaim are independent messages whose order is not controlled by either client.
suggestedResolution: Define: reclaim may clear ready ONLY if `session.state.activeTeam==='red'` AND no phase-end transition is in flight (the latch from the phase-end finding). If the phase already ended, the reclaiming human inherits the passed state and spectates until the next player phase (the combined design's 'acceptable and simple' default). State this as a hard rule, not an optional refinement, and route it through the single phase-end evaluator.

issue: `startNewRun()` / shared global `explored_hexes` + `runs` with no room scoping means two concurrent rooms write the same global tables (combined design §8 explicitly accepts 'shared overworld is global' for v1). But the per-room `runId` is the scoping key the new db.ts functions take (`saveExploredHex(runId, coord)`). Combined design §8 says the schema is UNCHANGED (still global, no run_id column) while the testing/overworld designs say db.ts gains a `run_id` column and run-scoped queries. If the schema stays global but two rooms each call `startNewRun()` and `saveExploredHex`, room B's exploration appears in room A's map; if the schema is run-scoped but `loadHexMapFromDb` seeds per-run, a fresh room starts blank. The two halves of the design contradict on whether the world is shared-global or run-scoped, and the race (two rooms exploring the same hex 'first') has opposite outcomes under each.
rationale: Combined design §8 keeps the schema global and explicitly flags concurrent-rooms cross-contamination as a med risk; the overworld and testing designs assume a run_id column and per-run isolation. These cannot both be true. Under the global-schema reading, concurrent rooms genuinely corrupt each other's overworld.
suggestedResolution: Decide run-scoping NOW since it changes the migration and every db.ts signature. Recommend adding `run_id` to explored_hexes/runs (the overworld design's migration) so concurrent rooms are isolated; do not ship the 'global shared world + multiple rooms' combination, which has no race-free reading.

issue: Action authorization checks `action.entityId === seat.heroEntityId` but `heroEntityId` is `null` in lobby and only set 'when entities are built at combat start' (seat §2). During the combat-start await window (encounter build yields the loop), an `action` message can arrive for a seat whose `heroEntityId` is still null OR whose hero entity does not yet exist in `session.state`. The guard `action.entityId === seat.heroEntityId` either rejects-with-ack against a null (fine) or, if `heroEntityId` was assigned before `session` was populated, forwards an action to a session whose entities map lacks that id, hitting `resolveAction`'s `NO_CHANGE` path silently — but the no-op ack sends `session.serialize()` of a possibly-half-built or previous session.
rationale: The order of (assign heroEntityId, create session, set phase=combat, broadcast combatStart) across the await is unspecified. The existing no-op ack path (index.ts:438) serializes whatever `session` currently is, which during the build gap could be the prior session.
suggestedResolution: Assign `heroEntityId` and populate `room.session` atomically with the synchronous phase flip before the build await completes, or reject `action` whenever `room.phase!=='combat' || room.session===null`. Ensure the no-op ack reads the same session generation that validated the action.

issue: `lastActivityMs` idle-reaping (combined design §7) and the all-AI room-reap timer (overworld §6) run on a wall-clock timer (`setTimeout`), which is a separate event-loop entry from message handling. The reaper calls `rooms.remove(id)` / `markRunInactive` and frees the code. A reclaim/join message for that exact room can be processed in the same tick window after the reaper decided to remove but before/after it mutates the maps, or a player reconnecting via `roomByToken` finds the token mapping still present but the Room already removed (dangling pointer → crash or silent new-room creation).
rationale: The reaper is timer-driven (async) and mutates the same `rooms`/`byCode`/`roomByToken` maps that reconnect reads/writes. With no generation/liveness check, a reconnect racing the reaper can dereference a removed room.
suggestedResolution: On reconnect, treat a `roomByToken` hit whose Room is missing/removed as 'no room' (fall to lobby) rather than dereferencing. Have the reaper re-check 'zero connected humans' atomically at fire time (not at arm time) and clear `roomByToken` entries for the room in the same synchronous block as `rooms.remove`.

issue: playerToken is fully spoofable: no server-minted secret, so any client can claim another player's seat by sending that player's token. The four designs treat playerToken as the sole identity primitive ('the wire never trusts a client claim of who I am beyond the token binding', wire-protocol §0) but the token is a client-generated UUID persisted in localStorage and sent verbatim in `hello`/`reclaim`. The server never issues or signs it. An attacker who learns/guesses a victim's token (it is just a UUID, but it is shared with the server in plaintext over a `ws://` URL query param in the current `?token=` handshake, and is reused on every reconnect) can send `reclaim {token: victimToken}` and the seat-design §4 reclaim logic will hand them the victim's seat mid-battle ('Find a seat where controller.reclaimToken === ws.playerToken. Match means this human owned it'). There is no check that the original socket is still the live owner before transferring.
rationale: The reclaim path in seat-design §4 and wire §9 unconditionally rebinds the seat to whichever socket presents a matching token, with no defense against a second client presenting the same token. Combined with token-in-query-string (logged by proxies, visible in browser history) this is a practical seat-hijack, not just theoretical.
suggestedResolution: Server must mint the authoritative token (or an HMAC-signed session token) in the `welcome`/`hello` ack and store the binding server-side; the client's localStorage token is only an *opaque handle* the server maps to a server-side secret. On reclaim, additionally require that either (a) the existing seat's socket is dead/disconnected, or (b) the reclaim carries a one-time reclaim-secret that was issued only to the original owner. Reject reclaim of a seat whose socket is still live (the 'token whose seat is still occupied by a live human -> rejected' negative test in the test-design is the right intent — make it a hard server rule, not just a test).

issue: Two browser tabs with the same token are not handled coherently and the four designs contradict each other. Seat-design §7 says 'a token can occupy at most one seat; on reclaim we also clear any stale seat binding for that token elsewhere', but wire-design §9 auto-reclaims on every `hello` ('if the token is already bound to a seat in a live room, the server auto-rejoins'). If a user opens a second tab (same localStorage token), tab 2's `hello` will auto-reclaim tab 1's live seat, silently kicking tab 1 to a dead socket — or, if both tabs are in different rooms, the 'clear any stale seat binding elsewhere' rule will yank the player out of room A when they open room B. There is no defined behavior for 'same token, two live sockets, same room'.
rationale: localStorage is shared across tabs of the same origin, so duplicate-token tabs are the *common* case, not an edge case. The auto-reclaim-on-hello rule (wire §2/§9) means the second tab steals the seat from the first with no warning, and the first tab's socket is now orphaned (its `ws.data.seatId` still points at a seat now owned by tab 2).
suggestedResolution: Define a single owner-socket invariant: a seat's `controller.socket` is the *only* live socket; on a new `hello`/`reclaim` for a token whose seat already has a live, responsive socket, either (a) reject the new connection with a typed error ('SEAT_IN_USE') and let the user explicitly force-takeover, or (b) gracefully close the old socket and transfer, but send the old socket an explicit `{type:'displaced'}` message so its UI shows 'opened elsewhere' rather than silently freezing. Pick one and document it in all four designs (currently they disagree).

issue: Reconnect arriving mid-defend-prompt has an unresolved double-resolution / lost-input race that the designs acknowledge but do not actually close. Combat-design §6.3 builds a DefendRound whose `expected` set excludes bot seats (auto-resolved synchronously at round build). When a human disconnects, their seat flips to bot (seat-design §4), so a defend round built *after* disconnect auto-resolves that target and removes it from `expected`. But if the human reconnects between round-build and round-completion, wire-design §9 says 'the bot hands control back at the next action opportunity; if a defend prompt is outstanding for that seat it is re-sent to the now-live socket (or had already been bot-auto-resolved — whichever happened first wins)'. The 'whichever happened first wins' is not deterministic across the two code paths: the round may have already auto-filled `results[target]` AND removed the seat from `expected`, yet wire-design also says to re-send the prompt to the reconnected socket, which would then accept a `defendResult` for an already-resolved target — either double-applying or being silently dropped with the client left waiting.
rationale: The current `AiTurnRunner.resolveDefend` (ai-turn-runner.ts:68-89) builds the defenseMap from whatever results arrive and calls `resolveAction` exactly once; there is no idempotency guard keyed to a promptId in the actual code. The designs add `promptId` and an `answered` flag (wire §7, combat-design risk mitigation) but the seat-design's reclaim path and the combat-design's auto-resolve path both write to the same DefendRound without a single authority deciding 'this target is already answered, reject further input.' The two designs were written by different subsystems and neither owns the tie.
suggestedResolution: Make the DefendRound the single authority for per-target resolution state with an explicit per-target status (`pending | answered`). On disconnect-flip-to-bot, do NOT auto-resolve a target that is mid-prompt; instead keep it `pending` and start a defend-timeout timer. On reconnect, only re-send prompts whose target is still `pending`. A `defendResult` for a non-`pending` target is rejected with an ack (no double-apply). The bot-auto-resolve should only fire on the timeout, not synchronously at round build, so there is exactly one resolution moment per target.

issue: Reconnect mid-AI-turn (mid bot burst / mid enemy phase) can desync because the designs apply controller swaps at 'message boundaries' but the bot burst and the AI step loop are synchronous while-loops that do not yield. Combat-design §5.4 runs the player-bot burst inside the existing `driveAiSteps` while-loop with `aiPlayerBusy=true`, and the overworld-design risk explicitly notes 'doing it during an in-flight AI step could double-drive a hero.' But `driveAiSteps` (index.ts:184-208) is a tight synchronous loop with no `await` between steps, so a reconnect `hello` message physically cannot be processed until the loop returns — meaning the mitigation ('mutate occupant + heroBrains only between steps / at message boundaries') is automatically satisfied for the *burst*, BUT the enemy phase can PAUSE on a `defendPrompt` (returns from the loop), at which point a reconnect IS processed, and if it flips a seat's controller and removes/installs a brain while a DefendRound is outstanding for the enemy phase, the subsequent `resolveDefend` resumes `driveAiSteps` with a mutated seat set. No design specifies that the runner's `entityQueue`/`pendingDefend` are revalidated against the live seat controllers after a reconnect during a defend pause.
rationale: The enemy phase legitimately yields control to the message loop at every defendPrompt (the only async pause point), and that is exactly the window a flaky-connection reconnect lands in. The runner keeps mutable `entityQueue`/`currentActions`/`actionIndex`/`pendingDefend` fields (ai-turn-runner.ts:23-29) that are not re-derived from current seat state on resume.
suggestedResolution: On any controller swap (disconnect or reclaim) that occurs while `session.pendingDefend` is set, do not mutate the in-flight DefendRound's already-`expected` seats; recompute only the routing (who gets the re-sent prompt) from current controllers. Add an explicit guard: a reclaim during enemy phase only changes who *future* prompts route to, never the current paused attack's resolution. Document that the player-bot burst window is reconnect-safe by virtue of the synchronous loop, but the enemy-phase defend pause is NOT, and needs the explicit rule above.

issue: Host disconnect host-migration has a split-brain and a host-powers gap. Seat-design §4 (lobby) says 'if host left but others remain, transfer hostSeatId to the lowest-index connected human', and (map/combat) says host 'becomes a bot like any seat; host powers (Start/reset) transfer to the new host.' But the in-combat path in §4 only specifies flipping the seat to a bot and does NOT actually re-run host migration — it only mentions migration in the lobby branch. Meanwhile the overworld-design's `Room` carries a `readonly hostToken` (overworld §1a), which is immutable, so host migration cannot even be expressed in that design's data model. The seat-design uses a mutable `hostSeatId`. These two designs model host identity incompatibly (immutable token vs mutable seatId), and neither defines what happens if the *new* host is itself a disconnected-bot seat (lowest-index connected human may not exist if all remaining humans also dropped during the same network blip).
rationale: Two of the merged designs disagree on whether host is `readonly hostToken` (overworld) or mutable `hostSeatId` (seat-design). If host is readonly, the disconnected host's room is permanently un-startable / un-resettable. The in-combat host-disconnect path in seat-design §4 omits the migration step that the lobby path has, so a host dropping mid-combat may leave `reset`/`debugWin`/Start-after-retreat with no valid owner.
suggestedResolution: Pick one host model: a mutable `hostSeatId` that always points at a currently-connected human seat (or null when none are connected). Make host migration a single function called on EVERY human disconnect regardless of phase, and on reconnect (a returning original host could optionally reclaim host, or not — decide). When no connected human remains, set `hostSeatId = null` and have host-gated messages (`startGame`, `reset`, `debugWin`) no-op with a typed error; the room then relies solely on the idle reaper. Reconcile the `readonly hostToken` in overworld §1a to a mutable field.

issue: Room code collision and expiry are under-specified and the two code-format definitions conflict. Seat-design §1 specifies a 4-char code from a 32-char alphabet (~1M codes) with `freshCode()` retrying until unused; wire-design §1 specifies a 4-6 char code. With a 4-char code and `freshCode` retrying on collision, there is no upper bound check — if the code space fills (or an attacker spams `createRoom` to exhaust it), `freshCode` loops forever or the registry rejects creation with no defined error. More importantly, codes are freed on `rooms.remove` (seat-design §1 RoomRegistry) but the idle reaper that triggers removal runs after 'N minutes' (seat-design §7) — so a code typed by a late-joining player can resolve to a *different* room than intended if the original room was reaped and the code recycled to a new room in the interim. There is no code TTL or generation counter to detect 'this code now points at a different room than when you copied it.'
rationale: 4 chars over a 32-symbol alphabet is only ~1.05M codes; with retry-on-collision and no creation cap, this is both a DoS vector and a recycle-confusion vector. Reaped-then-recycled codes mean a shared invite link/code can silently drop a player into a stranger's room.
suggestedResolution: Unify on one code format (recommend the longer 6-char form for headroom). Cap `freshCode` retries and return a typed `ROOM_CREATE_FAILED` error if exhausted. Add a per-room creation rate limit per token/IP. Either (a) never recycle a code while any client might still hold it (keep reaped codes in a quarantine TTL longer than the idle window), or (b) make join validate a room *generation* nonce embedded alongside the code so a stale code resolves to 'room expired' rather than a different live room.

issue: A player joining a room whose run is already in progress is handled inconsistently across the three relevant designs, with a capacity/seat-count contradiction. Seat-design §3.4 says join is only allowed in lobby unless a seat with this token exists (reclaim); wire-design §3 says `joinRoom` on a started room returns `ALREADY_STARTED` unless the token matches a seat (then it is reclaim). But overworld-design §6 'Join mid-run' says 'if found but a free human-capacity seat exists and !room.started, assign the lowest free seat' — yet capacity at Start is fixed by filling empties with bots (seat-design §3.5), so post-Start there are NO open seats (they are all human or bot), making 'a free human-capacity seat' impossible post-Start. The three designs agree post-Start join is reclaim-only, but none defines what happens when a NEW player (token never seen) tries to join an in-progress room: silent error, spectator, or queue? The test-design assumes 'spectator' for unknown-token reconnect to a full room, but no design actually implements spectator seats.
rationale: The test-design (§2.3 Step H negative) asserts 'unknown token to a full room -> spectator/error not seat-steal', but the spectator concept appears nowhere in the seat/wire/overworld data models — there is no non-seat observer in `Room.seats`. So the asserted behavior is untestable against the designed structures; a new joiner to an in-progress room has no defined landing state.
suggestedResolution: Explicitly decide and document: a new (unknown-token) join to a started room returns `ALREADY_STARTED`/`IN_PROGRESS` and the socket is left room-less (must create/join elsewhere). If spectating is desired, add an explicit spectator list to `Room` (not a seat) with read-only broadcast and no seatId — otherwise remove the spectator language from the test-design so it doesn't assert un-implementable behavior.

issue: Reclaiming a seat whose bot is mid-action: the brain-drop is racy because the bot's planned action list is already loaded into the runner before reclaim. When a human reclaims a bot seat during the player phase, seat-design §4 / overworld §6 'remove the Sovereign brain for seat.heroId from session.heroBrains so the human takes back control.' But `AiTurnRunner.loadActionsForNextEntity` (ai-turn-runner.ts:136-166) snapshots the brain's full `PlayerAction[]` into `this.currentActions` at the start of that entity's turn; dropping the brain from `heroBrains` does NOT clear `currentActions`/`actionIndex`. If the player-bot burst is mid-way through this seat's multi-action plan when reclaim lands (only possible if the burst yielded — but per combat-design the burst is synchronous and reclaim can only land after it, so for the burst this is safe), the larger risk is the *next* player phase: combat-design §5.3/§5.4 collects `botSeatIds` from `seats.filter(s => s.control.kind==='ai')` at `startPlayerPhase`; if reclaim flips the seat to human between phases but the brain wasn't actually removed from `heroBrains` (the map and the seat.controller are two separate stores that must be kept in sync), the runner would still drive a now-human seat as a bot, double-driving the hero.
rationale: `heroBrains` (a Map<EntityId, HeroController> on the session, encounter-session.ts:36) and `seat.controller.kind` (on the Room) are two separate sources of truth for 'is this hero bot-driven.' The designs require both to be mutated together on every human<->ai swap, but `loadActionsForNextEntity` keys off `heroBrains` while the burst-seat selection keys off `seat.controller.kind`. A missed mutation in either store on reclaim/disconnect double-drives or fails to drive a hero.
suggestedResolution: Make `heroBrains` membership a pure derivation of `seat.controller.kind` rather than an independently mutated map: at `startPlayerPhase` and `startEnemyPhase`, rebuild `heroBrains` from the current seat controllers (set brain iff `controller.kind==='ai'`, delete otherwise) so the two stores cannot drift. On reclaim, also clear any cached `currentActions` in the runner if the reclaimed entity is the one currently being driven (guard by `if entityId === currentlyDrivenId: abort its remaining queued actions`).

issue: Capacity edge cases (3rd/5th joiner) have an off-by-one and a TOCTOU race in the capacity check. Seat-design §3.4 computes capacity by 'count seats whose controller.kind === human' and takes the lowest-index open (bot, reclaimToken===null) seat, else FULL. But open slots are modeled as `controller.kind === 'ai'` with `reclaimToken===null` (seat-design §3.3), and bot-FILLED-at-Start slots are ALSO `controller.kind === 'ai'` — distinguished only by being post-Start. In lobby, a 'capacity 2' room has seat0=human + seat1=open-bot; the human-count is 1, so a join is allowed into seat1 (correct). But two clients sending `joinRoom` for the SAME last open seat concurrently both pass the 'open seat exists' check before either flips it (Bun ws message handling is single-threaded per the test-design risk note, so this is serialized — but only if both messages hit the SAME server process; the design has no statement about single-process, and the capacity check reads-then-writes across an `await buildDefaultInventory`-style boundary). Additionally, the 5th-joiner case for a capacity-4 room is only an error if capacity is enforced; seat-design §7 validates `2<=capacity<=4` at create, but the join handler counts humans not total seats, so if a disconnected human seat (still kind human, conn 'disconnected') is counted, a capacity-4 room with one dropped human blocks a legitimate join even though a seat is effectively free.
rationale: The disconnected-human seat occupies `controller.kind==='human'` (seat-design §4: disconnect flips to bot only in map/combat; in LOBBY it frees to open-bot — but mid-run a disconnected human stays kind-human-conn-disconnected in some readings). The join capacity check counts `kind==='human'` which conflates connected and disconnected humans, and the open-vs-filled-bot ambiguity (both are kind ai) means the 'is this seat joinable' predicate ('phase is lobby and kind ai') wrongly treats a Start-filled bot seat as joinable if phase somehow reads lobby.
suggestedResolution: Use an explicit `SeatState` enum (`open | human-connected | human-disconnected | bot-filled`) rather than overloading `controller.kind` + `reclaimToken` + `conn`. Capacity = total seats; joinable-open = `state===open` only. Compute the join target atomically in the message handler with no `await` between the check and the seat flip (do async inventory build into a local, then commit synchronously). Reject the 5th joiner via total-seat-count, and explicitly decide whether a mid-run disconnected human seat is reclaim-only (yes) so it never appears as 'open' to a new joiner.

issue: Cleanup of empty/abandoned rooms leaks the token->room index and has no teardown of in-flight timers. Seat-design §7 and overworld §6 arm an idle/reap timer when zero connected humans remain, removing the room. But: (1) `roomByToken` (overworld §1b) maps every player's token to the room code; on reap, the design says 'delete the room + roomByToken entries' but a disconnected-then-reaped player's token entry, if not removed, will on reconnect resolve to a dead room code and crash or mis-route. (2) The movement-vote timeout timer (overworld §5, `setTimeout` armed to deadline) and the defend-round timeout (combat §6.5) are per-room timers that are not listed in the reap teardown — a room reaped mid-vote or mid-defend-round leaves an orphaned timer holding a reference to the freed Room, preventing GC and potentially firing `tryResolve` on a removed room. (3) The reap timer itself races with reconnect: a player reconnecting in the window between 'last human left' and 'reap fires' must cancel the reap timer, but no design states the reap timer is cancelled on reclaim.
rationale: Three separate per-room timers (idle-reap, vote-timeout, defend-timeout) are introduced across the designs but only the idle-reap's *arming* is specified, never the coordinated teardown. Orphaned timers firing on removed rooms is a concrete crash/leak; an un-cancelled reap timer can delete a room a player just reconnected to.
suggestedResolution: Give `Room` an explicit `dispose()` that clears ALL its timers (reap, vote, defend) and removes every `roomByToken` entry for its seats, called exactly once from `rooms.remove`. On reclaim/reconnect that re-populates a room, cancel the pending reap timer. On reap, iterate `room.seats` and delete each seat's token from `roomByToken` (don't rely on a single host entry). Add an assertion that `tryResolve`/defend-resolve no-op if `rooms.get(code)` no longer returns this room instance.

issue: State-machine gap: the human<->ai seat controller transition has no defined 'ready/passed' carry-over rule, creating a phase-end deadlock or premature-flip on disconnect/reclaim. Combat-design §3.3 computes playerPhaseDone as 'every red seat is effectively ready (ready || dead || !affordable).' On disconnect mid-player-phase, seat-design §4 flips the seat to bot and 'the turn engine will now drive it via the brain and auto-ready it.' But the bot burst runs only at `startPlayerPhase` (combat-design §5.4), NOT when a seat flips to bot mid-phase — so a human who disconnects after the burst, while their seat is NOT yet ready, leaves a bot-controlled seat that never gets driven (the burst already ran) and never auto-readies, deadlocking playerPhaseDone forever. Conversely, on RECLAIM mid-phase, seat-design §4 and combat-design §5.4 disagree on whether to clear `ready`: §4 says 'only clear seat.ready if the player phase is still open and the hero has affordable actions; otherwise leave ready' (an OPEN_Q), while combat §5.4 says reclaim 'clears ready (if the bot hadn't acted yet).' If the bot already acted and set ready, but the human reclaims and the policy leaves ready=true, the human silently cannot act (spectates) — and if the policy is wrong and clears ready after the bot already consumed the hero's energy, the human reclaims an exhausted-but-not-ready hero, also unable to act, but now blocking nobody (exhausted counts as ready) — except the UI shows them as able to act. The transition table for {disconnect, reclaim} x {before-burst, after-burst, mid-defend, exhausted} is not enumerated anywhere.
rationale: This is the single most load-bearing state machine in the merge and it is left as an OPEN_Q in two designs ('Should reclaiming a seat mid-player-phase clear seat.ready'). The mid-phase disconnect deadlock is concrete: the bot burst is a one-shot at phase start (combat §5.4 step 1-4), so a seat that becomes a bot AFTER the burst has no mechanism to act or auto-ready, and playerPhaseDone (combat §3.3) requires every seat ready. The phase never ends.
suggestedResolution: Define the full transition table explicitly. Minimum fixes: (1) When a seat flips human->bot mid-player-phase (disconnect), immediately drive that single bot seat via `runHero(entityId)` (the seat-design §5 entry point exists precisely for this) and set `ready=true` afterward — do NOT rely on the start-of-phase burst. (2) On bot->human reclaim mid-open-phase: clear `ready` iff the hero still has an affordable action AND the bot has not already consumed its turn this phase; track a per-seat `actedThisPhase` flag so 'bot already acted' is distinguishable from 'never acted.' (3) Make `exhausted` always recomputed on reclaim so a reclaimed-exhausted hero is shown as non-actionable in the HUD. Resolve the contradicting OPEN_Qs in seat-design and combat-design to a single rule and put the transition table in the shared protocol doc.

issue: Token-in-query-string handshake (current `?token=` and `?dim=` upgrade path) versus token-in-first-message (`hello`) is inconsistent across designs, and the upgrade-time data construction can race the hello. Seat-design §3.2 reads `?token=` on the `/ws` upgrade; wire-design §2 and overworld §1c send the token in a `hello` MESSAGE after open ('the token must arrive in a message because the upgrade data is built from query params'). The current code (index.ts:233-245) builds all SocketData at upgrade time. If the merge keeps the upgrade-time path for token but the message-time path for create/join intent, there is a window where `ws.data.playerToken` is set but `roomId/seatId` are null and a stray `action`/`equip` message (or a reconnect storm) arrives before `hello` is processed — the message router (seat-design §6) resolves `room = rooms.get(ws.data.roomId)` = undefined and must no-op, but no design specifies the server rejects gameplay messages before the room/seat handshake completes. Combined with the query-param token, this also leaks the identity secret into server access logs.
rationale: The four designs use two different handshake mechanisms (query param vs first message) and do not reconcile which is authoritative. Query-string tokens are logged by Bun's access logging, reverse proxies, and browser history, undermining the token-as-secret model that the reclaim path depends on.
suggestedResolution: Standardize on token-in-first-`hello`-message only; do NOT read `?token=` at upgrade (keep `?dim=` only as an asset-preload hint, never as identity). At upgrade, set `ws.data = {playerToken:'', roomId:null, seatId:null}`. Make the message router hard-reject every gameplay/seat-scoped message with a typed error until `hello` has bound the socket to a token and a room. Never put the token in a URL.

issue: The four sub-designs CONTRADICT each other on whether per-seat ready/connection/phase/defend state travels INSIDE the serialized GameState snapshot or in a SEPARATE message. Design #2 (Wire Protocol) adds `coop: CoopMeta` (seats[], passed, exhausted, connected, pendingDefends, phase) as a field ON GameState and routes it through `state`. Design #3 (Combat) explicitly REFUSES this: 'ready flags do NOT go in GameState' and invents a separate `phaseStatus` message. Design #1 (Seat/Room) invents yet another `partyState` message, and Design #5 (Testing) assumes both `phase` messages AND coop-in-state. These cannot all be built; whichever loses, the other half of the system reads a field that does not exist and silently desyncs (clients show stale ready/waiting/defend status).
rationale: This is the single biggest divergence risk. The client designs (#4) gate `isPlayerPhase`/`canMyHeroAct`/`canUseAbility` on `state.phase` and `seat.passed`/`seat.exhausted` read from `coop`. If the server instead ships those in `phaseStatus`/`partyState` (per #3/#1), the client's `combat-ui-state.ts` reads `undefined` and every seat renders as actionable or none do. The two channels also race: a `state` snapshot and a separate `phaseStatus` are two messages with no atomic ordering guarantee, so a client can briefly apply a new board with old ready flags (or vice-versa), flickering 'waiting on X' and mis-enabling the Pass button.
suggestedResolution: Pick ONE carrier before any code lands. Recommended: put seat orchestration metadata in the GameState snapshot (Design #2's `coop`) so a single message is atomic and the 'client is a pure reconciler of one object' invariant holds. Then DELETE the `phaseStatus`/`partyState`/`defendWaiting`-as-separate-message paths from #1/#3, or explicitly demote them to lobby-only (pre-combat) where no `state` exists. Document that combat-phase seat status rides only in `coop`.

issue: `coop: CoopMeta` (Design #2) is claimed to 'pass through serialize/deserialize as plain JSON, no special handling needed,' but `shared/src/core/serialization.ts` does NOT pass arbitrary fields through. `serializeGameState` (lines 16-44) and `deserializeGameState` (lines 46-70) EXPLICITLY enumerate every field by name (entities, grid, mapDefinition, activeTeam, turnNumber, winner, nextSpawnId, actionCount, zones, nextZoneId). A `coop` field added to GameState would be silently dropped on serialize and absent on deserialize.
rationale: The design's own risk table rates this 'med, mitigated' on the false premise that serialization auto-passes new fields. It does not — it is a hand-written field-by-field copy. Adding `GameState.coop` without editing serialization.ts means the server's snapshot omits coop entirely and every client deserializes a GameState with no coop block, so all seat-aware UI breaks. The same is true for ANY new GameState field.
suggestedResolution: Edit `serialization.ts` to add `coop` to both `SerializedGameState`, `serializeGameState` (copy `state.coop`), and `deserializeGameState` (with a default like `data.coop ?? {phase:'player',seats:[],pendingDefends:[]}` for replay/back-compat). Add a round-trip test (Design #5's 'serialize → deserialize → serialize fixed point') that asserts coop survives. Note `controllerId` on Entity IS safe because entities are copied by reference whole (line 19 `entities[id] = entity`), so that claim alone holds.

issue: Full-snapshot-per-action does NOT scale and actively corrupts the free player phase with N humans. Every accepted `action` calls `broadcastState` which serializes the ENTIRE GameState (all entities + base64 walls + zones) to ALL seats. With 4 players each firing several abilities in a free phase, plus a bot burst, this is O(actions × players × full-state-size) of redundant payload. Worse: the design provides NO sequencing/version field on `state`, so out-of-order or coalesced WebSocket delivery cannot be detected by clients.
rationale: `server/src/index.ts:130-132` serializes the whole board on every change; `combat-store.ts:44-53` queues events>0 but for events.length===0 (the no-op ack path, used heavily for rejected cross-seat actions) it does `this.queue.length = 0` and snaps to newState. With multiple players acting concurrently, a no-op ack to player A (queue cleared, snap to latest) can DROP an in-flight animated update for player B's action that A had queued, because the queue is shared and cleared globally. `actionCount` exists on GameState but nothing uses it as a monotonic guard, so a late-arriving older snapshot silently overwrites a newer display state.
suggestedResolution: (1) Add a monotonic `actionCount`/sequence guard in `CombatStore.handleState`: ignore any snapshot whose actionCount < current display actionCount. (2) Reconsider the no-op ack: route per-seat rejections to a targeted `actionRejected{seatId}` message that unlocks ONLY that seat instead of broadcasting/snapping a full state that clears the shared animation queue for everyone. (3) For chattiness, gate that full snapshots are only sent to seats that need the board; consider event-only deltas for peers, but at minimum measure payload at N=4 before committing to full-snapshot-per-action.

issue: The burst-first bot ordering (Design #3 §5.4) introduces NON-DETERMINISTIC, observation-altering timing that diverges what different clients see, even though final state converges. Bots act in a synchronous server burst while `aiPlayerBusy=true` rejects human actions. Sovereign uses `Date.now()` budget and `Math.random()` (acknowledged). The ORDER and CONTENT of the bot burst depends on wall-clock and RNG at the instant the player phase starts — which depends on when the LAST human passed, which is network-timing-dependent.
rationale: Design #3 correctly argues resolver determinism is preserved (only the server runs Sovereign, broadcasts results). That is true for FINAL state. But the burst-first choice means: if a human reclaims a bot seat mid-phase (Design #1/#4), whether that seat's hero already acted depends on the Date.now-budgeted burst timing — a race the design hand-waves as 'short window.' Two reconnects milliseconds apart can land on opposite sides of the burst, producing different seat.ready states and different 'you reconnect to a passed seat vs an actionable seat' outcomes. This is the open-question 'should reclaim clear ready' surfacing as a real race, not a policy choice.
suggestedResolution: Make the bot burst a discrete, queued step sequence (not a synchronous while-loop) so reclaim can be evaluated at a well-defined step boundary, matching Design #2's risk-mitigation ('compute phase transitions only at well-defined points'). Define the reclaim-vs-burst race explicitly: if the bot's turn for that seat has not been APPLIED (not just planned), reclaim cancels the queued bot actions for that seat and clears ready. Document that bot decisions are server-only and never expected to reproduce; that part is fine.

issue: The concurrent-AoE defend round (Design #2 §7 / #3 §6) holds the enemy-phase AI runner PAUSED on a single `pendingDefend` while waiting for MULTIPLE human seats' `defendResult`s, but `AiTurnRunner` (ai-turn-runner.ts:28,68-89) only models ONE `pendingDefend` and resolves it with one `resolveDefend(results)` call. The Room must aggregate per-seat results before calling `resolveDefend` ONCE. If a disconnect/reconnect or timeout fires DURING aggregation, the partially-filled results map can be resolved twice or with a missing target.
rationale: `resolveDefend` (ai-turn-runner.ts:68) clears `this.pendingDefend = null` on entry and is not re-entrant. The designs add a Room-level `DefendRound` with `expected: Set<seatId>` and timeouts, but the timeout fires on a server timer (Date.now-based) — if a human's `defendResult` and the timeout's auto-fill both target the same seat in the same tick, the round can complete twice. The 'first answer wins, answered flag' mitigation (Design #2 risk) lives in the Room, not the runner, so the runner has no idempotency guard if the Room calls `resolveDefend` twice.
suggestedResolution: Make `resolveDefend` idempotent/guarded: it already early-returns `{done}` if `pendingDefend` is null, but the Room must ensure exactly one call. Give the `DefendRound` a single `resolved` boolean checked-and-set before calling `session.resolveDefend`. Add `promptId` to the runner's `pendingDefend` so a stale `defendResult` (from a reconnected socket re-sending) is matched and rejected. Test the timeout-races-with-last-human path explicitly (Design #5 lists it but as an assertion, not a guard).

issue: Animation/event ordering across multiple actors is undefined when multiple players' and bots' actions stream as separate `state` messages. `combat-store.ts` drains the queue gated on `_animatingCheck`, but the queue is a SINGLE global queue fed by interleaved broadcasts from different seats. There is no per-actor ordering or association of events to the seat that caused them, and the no-op ack path (events.length===0) CLEARS the entire queue mid-drain.
rationale: In single-player today, only one actor streams events so the queue is naturally ordered. With a free phase, player A's attack (events>0, queued and animating) can be followed by player B's no-op ack (events===0 → `this.queue.length = 0` at combat-store.ts:46) which DISCARDS A's not-yet-drained animation and snaps to the latest board, making A's hero teleport instead of animate. Conversely a slow animation on one client backs up the queue while the server has moved several actions ahead, so that client sees a growing lag and the 'waiting on X' overlay can be stale relative to the board it is rendering.
suggestedResolution: Stop clearing the whole queue on a no-op ack — a no-op ack for seat B must not flush seat A's queued animations. Either give no-op acks a dedicated non-state message, or have `handleState` only collapse the queue when the incoming snapshot's actionCount is strictly ahead of everything queued AND no animation is mid-flight. Tag each `state`/event batch so the client can drop superseded batches rather than blindly snapping.

issue: `shouldAutoEndTurn` (ability-cost.ts:30-38) and `resolveEndTurn`/`startTurn` (turn-resolver.ts:240-296) operate on the WHOLE active team at once, but the designs keep `activeTeam` flips server-INTERNAL and add a NEW per-seat exhaustion notion. The existing per-action auto-endTurn at index.ts:422-427 fires `endTurn` the instant the WHOLE red team has no affordable action — which under multi-hero red would end the shared player phase the moment ANY single hero exhausts the team-wide check, OR never (if it's recomputed per the new predicate). The two predicates (`shouldAutoEndTurn(state)` vs Design #3's per-seat `entityHasAffordableAction`) coexist and disagree.
rationale: Design #3 says to REMOVE the index.ts:422-427 auto-end and replace with the whole-phase `playerPhaseDone()`. But Design #5 (Testing) proposes KEEPING a 'back-compat endTurn resolver action' and the existing `shouldAutoEndTurn` for green tests. If both the old per-action auto-end AND the new phase predicate are live, a single human acting can trigger the old team-wide auto-end and flip red→blue while other seats still have energy and have not passed — ending the shared phase prematurely and stranding other players. This is a deadlock-or-premature-flip hazard the designs flag at high severity but resolve inconsistently.
suggestedResolution: Explicitly DELETE the index.ts:422-427 auto-end-after-action block in the refactor (Design #3 says so; make it a hard checklist item). Keep `shouldAutoEndTurn` ONLY as the energy/status regression-test shim for the back-compat `endTurn` action, never called in the live free-phase path. The live path must use the per-seat predicate exclusively. Add the integration test (Design #5 Step D) that a 1-human+3-bot phase does NOT flip until the human passes even if bots exhaust first.

issue: Reconnect/reclaim mid-combat sends the reconnecting client a full `state` snapshot with `events: []`, but `combat-store.ts:34-42` treats the FIRST state (displayState null) as instant and subsequent events===0 as a queue-clearing snap. A reconnect that arrives while the room is mid-animation on OTHER clients gives the reconnecting client a correct board, but the design never specifies that the server must send the LATEST authoritative state (not a mid-animation intermediate). If reconnect snapshots and live broadcasts interleave, the reconnecting client can bind its seat to a hero whose `controllerId`/entityId the client has not yet learned (heroEntityId is null until combat start and only in coop/roomState).
rationale: Open question in Design #4 itself: 'does the server resend the full event log or just the latest state on reclaim.' `CombatStore.handleState` relies on events.length===0 meaning 'instant snapshot,' which is the desired no-replay behavior — but only if the server guarantees it sends events:[] on reclaim AND the snapshot is the latest. The client's `autoSelectMyHero`/`myEntityId` resolves the seat's hero via `coop.seats`/`roomState.entityId === controllerId`; if the reclaim `state` arrives before `roomState`/`coop` is populated with this seat's heroEntityId, selection resolves null and the player is stuck spectating their own live hero.
suggestedResolution: Specify the reclaim sequence as ordered and atomic: server sends roomState/coop (carrying this seat's heroEntityId) BEFORE or WITHIN the same `state` snapshot, and the snapshot must be the current `session.serialize()` with events:[]. If coop rides inside GameState (recommended fix #1), this is automatic — the single snapshot carries both board and seat→hero binding. Confirm `events:[]` on reclaim so CombatStore snaps without replaying the whole fight.

issue: DB-backed shared overworld is GLOBAL (single `explored_hexes`/`runs`, no room_id), so two concurrent rooms cross-contaminate each other's exploration and run state. Multiple sub-designs acknowledge this but ship it as 'v1 accept one shared world,' while ALSO making `startNewRun()` per-room and `runId` per-Room. A per-room runId writing to a global `explored_hexes` keyed only by (q,r) (or even run_id in Design #1's migration) still lets Room A's `saveExploredHex` mutate the world Room B is reading if they share the global table without run scoping in the read path.
rationale: Design #1 migrates `explored_hexes` to include `run_id` and scopes reads by runId — good. But Design #5 and the Seat design (#1 top) keep `loadExploredHexes()` GLOBAL signature in places and the server index.ts:84-92 `loadHexMapFromDb` reads global. If the migration to run-scoped reads is incomplete (one caller still reads global), concurrent rooms desync their maps. The designs disagree on whether explored_hexes is run-scoped (overworld design) or global (seat design 'shared overworld is global').
suggestedResolution: Decide run-scoping ONCE. If per-room independent worlds are wanted, fully adopt Design #1's run_id migration and make EVERY read (`loadExploredHexes`, `loadHexMapFromDb`, `seedDiscovery`) take runId — audit for any remaining global-read caller. If one shared world is intended, do NOT make runId per-room for exploration purposes; keep a single world runId. The hybrid (per-room runId + global table) is the divergence trap.

issue: Movement vote determinism depends on the human electorate H, but H's definition (whether disconnected-but-still-human seats count) is left as an OPEN QUESTION across designs while the resolution math and tie-break are specified as 'deterministic.' If H is computed from `connected` humans on one path and from `controller.kind==='human'` (including disconnected→bot) on another, the same ballots resolve differently, and a disconnect during an open vote changes H mid-vote.
rationale: Design #1 §3.7 counts 'connected humans'; Design #2 §4 uses `humanSeatIds` electorate; Design #6 (overworld) open question explicitly says 'whether disconnected-but-still-human seats count toward H' is unresolved. The tie-break (proposer wins) and timeout (non-voters = abstain) are deterministic GIVEN a fixed H, but H is mutable by network events during the vote window. A disconnect that flips a human to bot mid-vote shrinks H and can instantly resolve a vote that was pending — a non-deterministic, timing-dependent outcome from the players' perspective, and clients computing the tally locally (vote-panel) will disagree with the server on the threshold.
suggestedResolution: Fix H's definition: count only CURRENTLY-connected human seats, recomputed on each ballot/disconnect, and make the server the sole authority on resolution (clients render `votes`/`humanSeatIds` from the server's `voteState` but never resolve locally — Design #4 already says client is reactive to `moveResolved`, enforce that no client computes the threshold). Snapshot H into the vote at proposal time OR recompute consistently on every event, and broadcast the electorate in `voteState` so clients display the same denominator the server uses.

issue: `controllerId` is added to `EntityCore` and the resolver is asserted to 'never read it,' which holds — BUT the client replaces ~15 `teamId==='red'` ownership checks with `controllerId===mySeatId`, and enemies/replays have `controllerId===undefined`. ReplayStore frames and any sim-built state have no controllerId, so `myHeroEntity`/`canMyHeroAct` return null and the HUD/ability-bar must degrade to spectator. Design #4 handles replay, but `ability-bar.ts:171` and `combat-ui-state.ts:22` currently hard-find the red entity; if any one site is missed in the sweep, a player with controllerId-based ownership and a fallback red-find will mis-target a DIFFERENT player's hero (the 'first red entity').
rationale: Verified: `combat-ui-state.ts:22` returns the FIRST live red entity and `ability-bar.ts:171` finds the FIRST red entity. In single-player there is one red hero so this is correct; with 2-4 red heroes, any un-migrated site silently controls/renders seat0's hero for everyone, a cross-control desync where players issue actions the server rejects (entityId controllerId mismatch) and the client UI shows the wrong hero's energy. The server rejects the action (good) but the client is stuck showing another player's hero — confusing and effectively unplayable for non-seat0 players until the sweep is complete.
suggestedResolution: Make the migration a hard grep-gate: every `teamId === 'red'` and `activeTeam === 'red'` site in client/ must be classified as either 'player phase live' (keep activeTeam check) or 'this is MY hero' (switch to controllerId===mySeatId), with NO first-red-entity fallback remaining. Enumerated sites to fix: combat-ui-state.ts:22,26; ability-bar.ts:171; client-state.ts selection reconcile; entity-renderer.ts:252 (cosmetic ring — add my-hero ring). Add a client unit test driving a 3-red-hero state asserting ability-bar reads MY seat's hero, not the first red.

issue: The four subsystem designs specify THREE mutually incompatible wire protocols for the same messages. SeatId format conflicts: subsystem 1 uses "seat0".."seat3", subsystem 2 uses "s0".."s3", subsystem 4 (client) uses "seat-0". Message names conflict: handshake is hello+welcome (subsys 2) vs hello+roomJoined (subsys 1/3) vs hello+assignSeat+roomState (subsys 4); combat start is combatStart (subsys 1/2) vs hexCombatStart (subsys 3/4); create reply is roomCreated+roomState (subsys 2) vs roomJoined (subsys 1). Defend: defendResult{seatId,promptId,power} (subsys 2) vs defendResult{results} (subsys 3) vs defendResult{promptId,results} (subsys 4). Implemented as four PRs, client and server will not speak the same protocol and the build/runtime is broken end-to-end.
rationale: shared/src/net/protocol.ts is named as the single source of truth (subsys 2) but subsys 1 and subsys 4 independently define overlapping DTOs (RoomStatePayload vs RoomStateDTO with different field names: yourSeatId vs mySeatId, kind vs controller). Subsys 4 lists defendResult{promptId,results} while subsys 3 (which owns the defend collection logic) specifies defendResult{results} with a single targetIds. These cannot both be true.
suggestedResolution: Before any implementation, reconcile all four MESSAGES/protocol sections into ONE canonical shared/src/net/protocol.ts as the first commit: one SeatId format, one message-name set, one defendResult shape. Subsys 2's catalog is the most complete; adopt it and force subsys 1/3/4 to import from it. Do not start coding until the catalogs are byte-identical.

issue: Fundamental disagreement: does combat/ready-phase state live IN GameState (serialized) or OUTSIDE it? Subsystem 2 adds `coop: CoopMeta` (phase, seats[], pendingDefends) and `CombatPhase` directly to GameState and routes ready/seat status inside the serialized snapshot. Subsystem 3 and subsystem 5 EXPLICITLY FORBID this: they keep ready flags only in server-side Room, carried by a SEPARATE phaseStatus message, and add only the single optional controllerId to Entity, leaving GameState/activeTeam untouched. Direct contradictions in the same shared/src/core/types.ts. The client (subsys 4) reads `state.phase` as if subsys 2 won.
rationale: Both designs claim authority over shared/src/core/types.ts with opposite changes. Subsys 3 says 'putting them in GameState would tempt resolver code to read them (breaking purity)'. Subsys 4's client reads state.phase (subsys 2 model) AND a phaseStatus message (subsys 3 model) in different sections. Whichever lands second will not compile against the first.
suggestedResolution: Pick ONE. Recommend subsystem 3's model (ready/phase in Room + phaseStatus message, only controllerId on Entity) because it preserves the verified-pure resolver and avoids editing every createGameState call site. Strike CoopMeta/GameState.coop from subsystem 2 and rewrite subsystem 4's client to read phaseStatus instead of state.phase.

issue: PlayerAction.endTurn deletion conflict. Subsystem 2 says 'remove {type:"endTurn"} from the PlayerAction union' in shared/src/core/types.ts. Subsystem 3, subsystem 5, and the AiTurnRunner all REQUIRE endTurn to stay: ai-turn-runner.ts:170 calls resolveAction(state,{type:"endTurn"}), the Room issues it as the phase-flip primitive, and 6+ turn-resolver tests (turn-resolver.test.ts:146,159,162,254,289,305,320) drive resolveAction(state,{type:"endTurn"}). Removing endTurn from PlayerAction breaks the resolver switch, the AI runner, and the existing test suite.
rationale: Verified types.ts:231-233 PlayerAction = ability | endTurn. Verified ai-turn-runner.ts:170 and turn-resolver.test.ts uses. sim-battle.ts:89 also handles action.type==="endTurn". Subsystem 2's deletion is incompatible with three other subsystems.
suggestedResolution: Keep {type:"endTurn"} in the PlayerAction union (server/AI-internal and test-load-bearing). Only stop ACCEPTING it from clients at the message router (server-side validation, not a type change). Correct subsystem 2's FILES note for types.ts.

issue: serialization.ts does NOT pass GameState through generically — it enumerates every field explicitly in serializeGameState (lines 27-43), deserializeGameState (lines 58-69), and the SerializedGameState interface (lines 3-14). Subsystem 2 claims 'serialization.ts already handles Map/Uint8Array and just needs to pass coop through (no special handling needed)'. False: a new GameState.coop would be silently DROPPED on serialize and undefined on deserialize unless all three sites are edited. (Entity.controllerId DOES round-trip automatically because entities are stored whole at lines 18/48 — that part is correct.)
rationale: Read serialization.ts directly: hand-written field-by-field copy, not a spread. Any GameState-level addition requires explicit edits to all three sites or it will not cross the wire. Compounds the subsys 2 vs subsys 3 disagreement; if subsys 3 wins (no GameState additions) this is a non-issue, reinforcing that choice.
suggestedResolution: If any GameState-level field is added, explicitly add it to all three sites in serialization.ts. Preferably avoid GameState additions entirely (favor subsystem 3) so only Entity.controllerId is added, which round-trips for free. Add a serialize->deserialize->serialize fixed-point test (subsys 5 plans one) to catch dropped fields.

issue: How a game BOOTS and how solo (1 human + bots) starts is under-specified, and the phase ordering leaves the build red. index.ts:118 has a top-level `let session = await EncounterSession.create(gameMode)` that the whole module depends on; subsystem 1 deletes the globals but no subsystem specifies the intermediate state where the server compiles with `rooms` while handlers/broadcasts still reference deleted `session`/`players`/`gameMode`. index.ts is a single ~520-line file with all handlers inline, so you cannot delete globals in one commit and add Room handlers in another without a red build. Solo is asserted as 'a room with 1 human + bots' but subsys 1 §7 enforces capacity 2..4, which conflicts with subsys 4/5 treating solo as capacity-4 with 3 bots; the lone-host Start path is undefined.
rationale: Verified index.ts:118 top-level await session create. Subsys 1 §7 'Capacity: 2 <= capacity <= 4, reject otherwise' vs subsys 4/5 defining solo as 1 human + 3 bots in a capacity-4 room. Unresolved, the lobby Start button for a lone host has undefined behavior.
suggestedResolution: Specify the canonical solo boot: host creates capacity-N (N>=2) room, presses Start immediately, server bot-fills seats 1..N-1, and Start is allowed with only the host present. Sequence the index.ts rewrite as ONE atomic commit (delete globals + add rooms + rewrite all handlers together) since the file is monolithic. Add a 1-human + N-bot solo integration test.

issue: Hero/loadout selection pre-game is an unresolved OPEN_Q in every subsystem (subsys 1, 2, 4) yet combat encounter build hard-depends on each seat's equipped/attachments/animSet. If lobby loadout editing is not built, 'full multiplayer in one PR' is incomplete: players cannot equip before the first fight and every seat is just STARTER_ITEM_IDS.
rationale: Verified equip is gated to non-combat phase and inventory auto-builds from STARTER_ITEM_IDS (index.ts:58-67). Subsys 4 §8 proposes a LobbyScreen loadout mode but lists it as design-assumed; the open question is unresolved across all four. A PR claiming completeness without lobby loadout editing is functionally incomplete for any non-default build.
suggestedResolution: Either (a) explicitly descope pre-game loadout for v1 (everyone STARTER_ITEM_IDS, equip only between encounters on the map) and state it clearly, or (b) commit to subsys 4's lobby loadout mode with concrete acceptance and add it to the file list. Do not leave it open if the PR claims completeness.

issue: Error/disconnect UX completeness gaps. protocolMismatch->close is specified server-side (subsys 2) but no client file is assigned to render the 'update your client' UI. Reconnect grace window (instant flip vs few-second hold) is an unresolved OPEN_Q in subsys 1; instant flip yanks a human's hero to a bot on a 1s blip mid-defend, which subsys 1/2 admit can desync the defend round. Defend-prompt server-authoritative timeout is an OPEN_Q in subsys 2 but asserted-as-built in subsys 3 §6.5 — another cross-subsystem inconsistency. An AFK human with energy who never passes stalls the whole party's player phase (subsys 3 RISK 'playerPhaseDone never becomes true').
rationale: Subsys 1 'reconnect grace window' open; subsys 3 §6.5 asserts defend timeout auto-fills neutral; subsys 2 lists the defend timeout as open. The terminal protocolMismatch path and the AFK auto-pass are flagged but not committed to a file.
suggestedResolution: Commit to: (1) server-authoritative defend timeout with a concrete deadline auto-resolving to neutral; (2) a per-seat AFK auto-pass timer in the player phase so one idle human cannot deadlock playerPhaseDone; (3) a concrete client handler+banner for protocolMismatch in main.ts; (4) a short 2-3s reconnect grace before human->bot flip. Assign each to a specific file.

issue: shared/package.json test script is `vitest run` (verified) and the 4 bun:test files cannot be resolved by vitest, so the suite is ALREADY partially red; the new server integration harness (Bun.serve + bun:sqlite + WebSocket) cannot run under vitest at all. Without migrating shared/package.json to `bun test` and adding a root test script, the new multiplayer tests have no runner and CI cannot gate the PR.
rationale: Verified shared/package.json test="vitest run". Verified 6 files import vitest, 4 import bun:test. Root package.json has no `test` script (only typecheck/lint/sim-battle). The harness must run under bun test; if missed, new tests are silently unexecuted.
suggestedResolution: Early commit: change shared/package.json test to `bun test`, migrate the 6 vitest imports to bun:test (API-compatible, no body changes), add root package.json `"test": "bun test"`. Keep `bun scripts/sim-battle.ts` as a CI exit-0 smoke gate. Make it a hard dependency of the PR, not optional.

issue: db.ts opens the sqlite at import time relative to cwd (db.ts:19 `new Database("hex-discovery.sqlite")`), and index.ts:37-42 runs seedDiscovery(15) + 5 seed functions at module load. Any server-side test importing the server entry or EncounterSession (transitively imports db.ts) writes to the dev DB and re-seeds. GAME_DB_PATH env refactor is a precondition for the harness; without it, tests clobber server/hex-discovery.sqlite (exists, 364KB). Also: run-scoped explored_hexes (overworld subsys drops/recreates at user_version<2) means the first real server start after merge WIPES existing exploration — acceptable per design but must be in the PR description.
rationale: Verified db.ts:19 module-level open, no env var. Verified index.ts:37 module-level seedDiscovery(15). Verified server/hex-discovery.sqlite exists with WAL. dimension-generator/auto/generate-spec.ts also opens the file readonly. The DROP TABLE migration and in-memory test isolation both depend on the GAME_DB_PATH refactor landing first.
suggestedResolution: Add `const DB_PATH = process.env.GAME_DB_PATH ?? "hex-discovery.sqlite"` to db.ts as an early commit. Harness sets GAME_DB_PATH=":memory:" before dynamic-importing the server (mirror sim-battle's chdir+dynamic-import). Guard the seedDiscovery/seed* calls behind an init function the harness can control so test imports don't force a reseed.

issue: placePvpEntities/placePveEntities deletion is safe but must be sequenced. Verified placePvpEntities/placePveEntities have NO importers outside encounter-session.ts:110, so they can be deleted once the duel/pvp/pve branches go. But scenarios.js is barrel-exported via shared/src/index.ts:29, and encounter-session.ts:10-11 imports them through the barrel — deleting the functions without rewriting create() in the SAME commit breaks the encounter-session compile. buildScenarioMap/createCombatGrid must be KEPT (sim-battle.ts:52, hero-arena/src/arena.ts:51, hero-arena/src/t2/arena2.ts:33 depend on them) — the designs correctly say keep these.
rationale: Verified placePvpEntities/placePveEntities importers: only encounter-session.ts:110. Verified buildScenarioMap importers: sim-battle, arena, arena2. Verified shared/src/index.ts:29 re-exports scenarios. encounter-session.ts:22 imports FIGHTER_WEIGHTS/TANK_WEIGHTS/RANGED_WEIGHTS/PRESETS/makeSovereign from hero-arena which the new sovereignFor() reuses — keep those.
suggestedResolution: Delete placePvpEntities/placePveEntities from scenarios.ts in the SAME commit that removes encounter-session's duel/pvp/pve branches. Keep buildScenarioMap, createCombatGrid. Keep the sovereign weight imports; only drop FIGHTER_TEMPLATE (encounter-session.ts:21) if the duel hardcoded roster is its sole user (verify).

issue: entity-factory.ts path and controllerId threading. The overworld subsystem flagged 'entity-factory.ts assumed; verify path' — it IS shared/src/encounter/entity-factory.ts (verified), and there is a SECOND makeEntity in shared/src/__tests__/test-helpers.ts. makeEntity (lines 5-14) does not take controllerId; encounter-builder.ts:37 sets playerAnimSet via post-build spread `{...playerEntity, playerAnimSet}`, NOT through makeEntity. The designs variously say add controllerId as a makeEntity arg (overworld) OR stamp via spread (subsys 1). Two different mechanisms; pick one.
rationale: Verified makeEntity signature ends at attachments (no controllerId) and there are two makeEntity definitions. Verified encounter-builder.ts:37 stamps playerAnimSet via spread, establishing the post-build-spread precedent. Spreading controllerId avoids editing makeEntity's many call sites (sim-battle, scenarios, test-helpers).
suggestedResolution: Stamp controllerId via the post-build spread in placeEncounterEntities (matching the playerAnimSet pattern) rather than adding a makeEntity parameter, to avoid touching makeEntity's call sites. Document that only player-team heroes get controllerId; enemies leave it undefined.

issue: ability-bar.ts:171 single-red-entity HUD assumption mis-renders with a 2-4 hero party. Verified `[...state.entities.values()].find(e => e.teamId === "red")` returns the FIRST red entity, so every client's HUD would show seat0's hero. This is one of ~15 hardcoded ==="red" sites; the risk is missing one and stranding input. The designs enumerate the sites but the actual count/locations should be grep-verified before claiming the sweep is complete.
rationale: Verified ability-bar.ts:171 finds first red entity (single-hero assumption). The combat/client designs replace this with state.entities.get(seat.myEntityId()). Verified client-state.ts:217 selection drops teamId!=="red". A missed site mis-routes or freezes a hero.
suggestedResolution: Grep-enumerate every `=== "red"` and `activeTeam` site (combat-ui-state.ts, client-state.ts:217, ability-bar.ts:171, entity-renderer.ts) and classify each as 'is-me' (-> controllerId===mySeatId), 'is-player-phase' (-> activeTeam==="red" kept), or 'friend/foe color' (kept). Add a client check confirming each of 2 humans sees THEIR own hero in the ability bar.
